essential_question: How do a shape's sides and angles pin down exactly which quadrilateral it is — and how do you measure the distance all the way around it?
objective: Identify polygons and the quadrilateral family (square, rectangle, parallelogram, rhombus, trapezoid) by their defining properties, compute the perimeter of simple and composite (L-shaped) figures, and state the interior angle sum of a triangle (180°) and a quadrilateral (360°).

content.prompt: A city block is basically a rectangle. If you walked its entire boundary once, what exactly would you be adding up? And here's the trickier one: is every square secretly also a rectangle — or are they two separate shapes that just happen to look alike?

agenda.title: I do — The Quadrilateral Family Tree

content.heading: Six shapes, one family tree, and the distance around
bullets: A polygon is a closed figure made of straight sides; a quadrilateral is any four-sided polygon at all.
Parallelogram = two pairs of parallel sides (angles may or may not be right angles). Rectangle = a parallelogram with four right angles. Rhombus = a parallelogram with four congruent sides.
Square = four congruent sides AND four right angles — so a square is a rectangle with congruent sides, and also a rhombus with right angles.
Trapezoid = only ONE pair of parallel sides — it can never also be a parallelogram, rectangle, rhombus, or square.
Perimeter is the distance around a figure — add every side. For a composite (L-shaped) figure, first find any missing side lengths, then add every side of the actual boundary.
The interior angles of a triangle always sum to 180°; the interior angles of a quadrilateral always sum to 360° — split any quadrilateral with one diagonal into two triangles to see why.

agenda.title: Explore — Drag a Corner, Watch It Reclassify

content.heading: Drag a corner; watch the name and the perimeter change
content.note: On the sim, drag any of the four gold corners. Watch the shape's name update live — try to make it read 'Square,' then 'Rectangle,' then 'Parallelogram,' then 'Rhombus,' then 'Trapezoid,' and finally a plain 'General quadrilateral.' Watch the perimeter number too — does it go up or down as you stretch a corner outward?

content.preview: Build session: measure real quadrilaterals' perimeters and build your own set from cardstock or straws.

success_criteria.text: I can identify a polygon and each quadrilateral family member (square, rectangle, parallelogram, rhombus, trapezoid) by its defining properties
I can find the perimeter of a simple figure and a composite (L-shaped) figure by finding any missing sides and adding all the sides
I can state the interior angle sum of a triangle (180°) and a quadrilateral (360°), and explain why a square is always a rectangle but not the reverse

questions.prompt: A quadrilateral with two pairs of parallel sides — angles may or may not be right angles — is a…
questions.explain: Two pairs of parallel sides, with no requirement about the angles, is exactly the parallelogram definition. A rectangle also has two pairs of parallel sides, but ONLY a rectangle requires all four angles to be right angles.

questions.prompt: The interior angles of ANY quadrilateral — square, kite, or any other four-sided figure — always add up to…
questions.explain: Draw one diagonal across any quadrilateral and it splits into two triangles. Each triangle's angles sum to 180°, and together they cover every interior angle of the quadrilateral exactly once: 180° + 180° = 360°.

questions.prompt: A rectangular garden plot measures 9 m by 5 m. Find its perimeter.
questions.explain: Perimeter = sum of all four sides: 9 + 5 + 9 + 5 = 28 m (or 2(9 + 5) = 28 m).

questions.prompt: A storage shed's footprint is a rectangle with one rectangular corner notch cut out (an L-shape), and every angle in the figure is a right angle. The overall length is 11 ft and the overall width is 7 ft. The notch removed from that corner is 4 ft by 3 ft. Find the two missing side lengths, then find the perimeter of the actual L-shaped footprint.
questions.answer: Missing sides: 7 ft (11 − 4) and 4 ft (7 − 3). Perimeter = 11 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 7 + 7 = 36 ft.
questions.explain: Rubric: correctly finds both missing sides by subtracting the notch from the overall dimensions (1 pt) + adds all six sides of the actual boundary for 36 ft (1 pt).

questions.prompt: A quadrilateral has four congruent sides, but its angles measure 100°, 80°, 100°, 80° — none of them right angles. What is the BEST name for this shape?
questions.explain: Four congruent sides with angles that are not right angles is exactly the definition of a rhombus. It would only be a square if all four angles were also right angles.

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw a quadrilateral that IS a parallelogram but is NOT a rectangle. Label it and write one sentence explaining how you know it's a parallelogram but not a rectangle.
questions.answer: Any slanted (non-right-angle) four-sided figure with two pairs of parallel sides, labeled, with a sentence noting both pairs of opposite sides are parallel but the angles are not 90°.
questions.explain: Rubric: a drawn figure with two pairs of parallel sides (1 pt) + a sentence correctly noting the angles are NOT right angles, which is what keeps it from being a rectangle (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Exit: find one quadrilateral-shaped object near you right now (a book cover, a window, a tile, a picture frame) and name the MOST SPECIFIC family member it belongs to. Then name one OTHER family name it also belongs to, and say why.
